content:
# Genesis Chapter 7 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 4v–5r | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_007_page_001.jpg, GEN_007_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0009

---

**1** Then the LORD said to Noah: "Go — you and all your household — into the ark. For I have seen that you are righteous before me in this generation.

**2** "From every clean animal, take with you seven pairs — a male and its female. From the animals that are not clean, take one pair — a male and its female. **3** From the birds of the sky as well, take seven pairs — male and female — to keep their kind alive on the face of all the earth.

**4** "For in seven more days I will send rain on the earth — forty days and forty nights. And I will wipe out from the face of the ground every living thing that I have made."

**5** And Noah did everything — exactly as the LORD commanded him.

---

**6** Noah was six hundred years old when the floodwaters came upon the earth.

**7** And Noah came in — and his sons, his wife, and his sons' wives with him — into the ark, before the waters of the flood.

**8** Of the clean animals and the animals that are not clean, of the birds and everything that crawls on the ground — **9** two by two they came to Noah, into the ark, male and female, just as God had commanded Noah.

**10** And after seven days the waters of the flood came upon the earth.

---

**11** In the six hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, on the seventeenth day of the month — on that very day —

all the springs of the great deep burst open,
and the floodgates of the sky were thrown wide.

**12** And the rain fell on the earth forty days and forty nights.

---

**13** On that very same day, Noah came in — and Shem, Ham, and Japheth, the sons of Noah, and Noah's wife, and the three wives of his sons with them — into the ark. **14** They came in, and every beast according to its kind, every animal according to its kind, every creature that crawls on the earth according to its kind, every bird according to its kind — every winged creature, everything that flies. **15** They came to Noah, into the ark, two by two — all flesh in which there was the breath of life. **16** Those that came, male and female from all flesh, came just as God had commanded him.

Then the LORD shut the door behind him.

---

**17** The flood came on the earth forty days. The waters increased and lifted the ark — and it rose above the earth. **18** The waters swelled and increased greatly upon the earth, and the ark moved across the surface of the waters. **19** The waters swelled more and more, greatly, upon the earth, until all the high mountains under all the sky were covered. **20** The waters prevailed fifteen cubits higher still — and the mountains were covered.

---

**21** And all flesh that moved on the earth perished —
bird and animal and beast and every swarming creature that swarms on the earth,
and every human being.

**22** Everything that had the breath of life in its nostrils —
everything on dry land —
died.

**23** He wiped out every living thing that was on the face of the ground:
human being and animal, crawling creature and bird of the sky —
they were wiped out from the earth.

Only Noah remained — and those with him in the ark.

**24** And the waters prevailed upon the earth a hundred and fifty days.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### On Genesis 7 as a whole — the un-creation

Genesis 7 is the chapter in which the world ends.

Not metaphorically. Not symbolically. The world ends. The creation that began with God separating the waters in chapter one — pushing the chaos back, ordering it, holding it in place with the firmament and the gathered seas — now comes undone. The separation fails. The waters return. Everything that breathes stops breathing.

The chapter is structured as an inhalation and an exhalation: preparation, then catastrophe. The first sixteen verses record the entry into the ark with meticulous, almost liturgical repetition — the animals coming in pairs, the family members named again, the seven days of waiting, the date recorded with archival precision. And then verse 11 explodes, and verses 17–24 are the flood itself, the water rising and rising, the death catalog accumulating, and then verse 24: silence. One hundred and fifty days. The waters prevailed.

The translator must feel the weight building through verses 17–20 — each verse adding more water, using the same word again and again (*swelled*, *increased*, *prevailed*) — and then the absolute crash of verses 21–23. The poetry of the death catalog is not ornamental. It is the text holding itself open to the enormity of what is being described.

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 4v–5r, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Three-column format with full Tiberian Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. Additional reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana.*

---

### Verse 11 — The springs of the great deep and the floodgates of the sky: un-creation

This is the pivot of the entire chapter. The date formula — the year, the month, the day, and then the phrase "on that very day" (בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה, *be'etsem hayyom hazzeh*), which marks the most solemn moments in the Hebrew calendar — introduces what follows with the weight of a declaration of judgment.

Then the cosmos comes apart.

**מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם רַבָּה (ma'ayanot tehom rabbah)** — "the springs of the great deep." The word תְּהוֹם (tehom, "deep") is the word of Genesis 1:2 — the formless void, the darkness over the face of the deep, the chaos that pre-existed creation. In the beginning, God separated the upper waters from the lower waters with a firmament (1:6-7), gathered the lower waters into seas (1:9-10), and placed the dry land where creatures could live. The deep was not eliminated. It was *contained*. Now it breaks out. The תְּהוֹם was always there, below and above, pressing against the created order. On the seventeenth day of the second month of Noah's six hundredth year, the containment fails.

**נִבְקְעוּ (nivqe'u)** — "burst open/were split." The verb בָּקַע (baqa') means to split, to cleave, to break through. It is the same verb used for the earth opening its mouth (Numbers 16:31), for the Red Sea splitting (Exodus 14:16), for the rock cracked open to give water in the desert (Numbers 20:11). This is not a gradual seeping. The springs of the deep *burst*. The word is violent. The deep does not leak — it erupts.

**אֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם (arubot hashamayim)** — "the floodgates of the sky / the windows of heaven." The word אֲרֻבָּה (arubah) means a window, a lattice, an opening — specifically the kind of opening through which things flow. It appears in 2 Kings 7:2 and 7:19 when the prophet promises such abundance that the very windows of heaven will open to pour grain down. Here they open for water. The firmament — the רָקִיעַ (raqia') of Genesis 1:6, the beaten-out expanse that held the upper waters back — has been unlatched. The water above the sky is coming through.

The cosmological picture is exact. On Day Two of creation, God made the firmament and "separated the waters that were under the firmament from the waters that were above the firmament" (1:7). On the seventeenth day of the second month of Noah's six hundredth year, both separations are undone simultaneously: the deep erupts from below and the sky opens from above. The creation of Days Two and Three is being reversed. This is not just a flood. This is un-creation.

The LXX Vaticanus renders: **ἐρράγησαν πᾶσαι αἱ πηγαὶ τῆς ἀβύσσου καὶ οἱ καταρράκται τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ἠνεῴχθησαν** — "all the springs of the abyss burst open, and the floodgates of heaven were opened." The Greek ἄβυσσος (abyssos, "abyss") preserves the weight of the Hebrew תְּהוֹם — the primordial deep, the chaos before creation. The word will appear in the New Testament's Book of Revelation (9:1, 11:7, 20:1) as the name of the realm of the demonic — the abyss, the bottomless pit, the un-created chaos underneath the order of the world.

---

### Verse 16 — "Then the LORD shut the door behind him"

וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה בַּעֲדוֹ

(*vayyisgor YHWH ba'ado*)

Five Hebrew words. The most tender moment in the chapter, and the most terrifying.

The structure of verse 16 up to this point has been bureaucratic: the animals entering two by two, male and female, "just as God had commanded Noah." The entrance into the ark has been orderly, complete, correct. And then, in the last five words of the verse, the narrative halts and does something unprecedented.

**God shuts the door.**

Not Noah. Noah did not shut the door. The LORD — יְהוָה, the personal name, the covenantal name — shut the door. This is the only time in the flood narrative that God performs a specific physical action from outside the ark after Noah has entered. The builder of the ark did not seal himself in. The one who commanded the building sealed it for him.

**וַיִּסְגֹּר (vayyisgor)** — "and he shut/closed/locked." The verb סָגַר (sagar) means to close, to shut, to deliver up, to hand over. It is used for shutting doors (Judges 3:23), for closing wombs (Genesis 20:18), for sealing up leprosy (Leviticus 13:4). It is final. What is closed by סָגַר is closed.

**בַּעֲדוֹ (ba'ado)** — "behind him / around him / for him." The preposition בְּ (in/with) combined with עַד (ad, up to, around, for the sake of) and the third person singular suffix: *behind him*, *around him*, *on his behalf*. The translation "behind him" captures the physical reality; "for him" captures the covenantal reality. Both are present in the Hebrew. The LORD shut the door *behind Noah* — and in doing so, shut it *for Noah's sake*.

What makes this the most tender moment in the chapter is what it implies about Noah's inability to save himself. He could build the ark. He could gather the animals. He could bring his family in. But he could not seal himself against the flood. The sealing required someone on the outside. The one who stood outside, who was about to send the waters that would kill every other living thing on earth, took a moment — before the rain — to shut the door behind the one man he had decided to save.

And then the waters came.

The tenderness and the terror are inseparable. The same action — the LORD closing the door — is simultaneously the act of love that preserves Noah and the act of judgment that seals the fate of everything outside. There is no way to have one without the other. The ark is only a shelter because there is a flood. The closed door is only safety because on the other side of it, everything is dying.

The LXX Vaticanus simply reads: **καὶ ἔκλεισεν κύριος ὁ θεὸς τὴν κιβωτὸν ἔξωθεν αὐτοῦ** — "and the Lord God shut the ark from outside him." It adds "from outside" (ἔξωθεν), making explicit what the Hebrew implies: God is *outside*. God shuts the door from where the flood will be. The intimacy of that position is almost unbearable.

---

### Verses 17–20 — The water rising: the rhetoric of accumulation

The flood narrative uses repetition as a formal device in a way that mirrors the creation narrative — but inverted. In Genesis 1, creation accumulates: light, firmament, sea and land, vegetation, lights, creatures, humans. Each day adds. In Genesis 7:17–20, destruction accumulates in the same rhythmic way:

- v. 17: The waters *increased* and lifted the ark
- v. 18: The waters *swelled* and *increased greatly*
- v. 19: The waters *swelled more and more, greatly* — until all the mountains were covered
- v. 20: The waters prevailed *fifteen cubits higher still*

The key verb is **גָּבַר (gavar)** — "to prevail / to be strong / to overwhelm." It first appears in verse 18 and will anchor the entire chapter's conclusion in verse 24: *the waters prevailed upon the earth a hundred and fifty days.* The word גָּבַר does not merely mean "to rise." It means to be mighty, to master, to dominate. The waters are not just high. They are sovereign. They have taken the earth and they will not give it back.

The phrase **עַל כָּל־הָרֵי הַגָּבֹהַ אֲשֶׁר תַּחַת כָּל־הַשָּׁמָיִם** — "all the high mountains under all the sky" — is maximalist to the point of insistence. Not some mountains. Not the highest mountains. *All the high mountains under all the sky.* The rhetorical reach is global. The narrator wants no escape clause. There was no high ground. There was no ridge where something survived. The text refuses the reader the comfort of an exception.

---

### Verses 21–23 — The death catalog

The three-verse catalog of death is the most formally deliberate passage in the chapter. The Hebrew moves through the categories of the living — bird, animal, beast, swarming creature, human being — and pronounces on each in turn: they perished. They died. They were wiped out.

**וַיִּגְוַע (vayyigva')** — "and they perished / expired / breathed their last." The verb גָּוַע (gava') is specifically the breath leaving a body — to expire, to gasp out, to die the death that comes when the breath stops. It is used repeatedly in the book of Job for the moment of death (Job 3:11, 10:18, 13:19, 14:10). The word is physiological: it is what happens in the lungs when life ends.

The catalog of verse 21 reverses the creation order of Genesis 1: birds first, then animals, then crawling creatures, then humans — inverting the sequence in which they were made. Creation is being taken back in something like the order it was given. The un-creation is liturgically careful.

**כֹּל אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁמַת־רוּחַ חַיִּים בְּאַפָּיו** — "everything that had the breath of life in its nostrils." The phrase **נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים (nishmat chayyim)** — "breath of life" — reaches back to Genesis 2:7, where God breathed into the human form the נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים and the human became a living soul. What God breathed in at the beginning, the flood has breathed out. The breath that animated the world has been extinguished. Every nostril, empty.

**מִכֹּל אֲשֶׁר בֶּחָרָבָה (mikkol asher becharavah)** — "everything on dry land." The word חָרָבָה (charavah) means dry ground — the ground that appeared when the waters were gathered in Genesis 1:9. The dry land was the stage on which the creatures lived. Now "dry land" is a category for a world that no longer exists.

Then comes the most spare and complete sentence in the chapter:

**וַיִּמַּח אֶת כָּל הַיְקוּם אֲשֶׁר עַל פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה**

"He wiped out every living thing on the face of the ground."

The verb **מָחָה (machah)** — "to wipe out / to blot / to erase" — is the same verb God used in chapter 6 when he announced his intent: "I will wipe out humanity from the face of the ground" (6:7). The announcement has now become the act. What was declared is done. The word machah means to erase as one erases writing, to wipe as one wipes a surface clean. The surface of the earth has been cleaned.

The sentence ends with absolute exclusion:

**אַךְ נֹחַ וַאֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ בַּתֵּבָה**

"Only Noah — and those with him in the ark."

The particle **אַךְ (akh)** is restrictive and absolute: *only*, *solely*, *nothing but*. It is the same word used in Genesis 6:5 (רַק, *raq*, "only evil") — but now the restriction runs the other direction. In chapter 6, the only thing left in the human heart was evil. Now the only thing left living on the face of the earth is Noah. The syntax is an inversion: where once *evil alone* defined the inner life of humanity, now *Noah alone* defines the outer face of the world. Two absolutes, one answering the other.

---

### Verse 24 — "The waters prevailed upon the earth a hundred and fifty days"

וַיִּגְבְּרוּ הַמַּיִם עַל הָאָרֶץ חֲמִשִּׁים וּמְאַת יוֹם

(*vayyigberu hammayim al ha'arets chamishim ume'at yom*)

The chapter ends without drama. No one speaks. Nothing moves. The waters simply prevail — and the number is given, and the text stops.

**וַיִּגְבְּרוּ (vayyigberu)** — "and they prevailed." The verb גָּבַר appears one final time. It has been building since verse 18. Now it is the only thing: the waters prevailed. No verb of destruction, no catalog of death, no divine speech. Just the waters, and the earth, and the number of days.

**חֲמִשִּׁים וּמְאַת יוֹם (chamishim ume'at yom)** — "a hundred and fifty days." Five months of water ruling the earth. The silence of those five months is the silence that falls after catastrophe — not peace, but the absence of any remaining sound to compete with the water's sovereignty.

The chapter ends with a world that has been remade into what it was before creation: water everywhere, no dry land, no breath in any nostril except in one wooden box floating in the dark. The creation narrative has been run to its close. The world that was made in six days has been unmade in six months.

And the LORD — who shut the door before the rain — is still there. Chapter 8 will begin: "And God *remembered* Noah." But that is chapter 8. This chapter offers no memory, no rainbow, no dove. This chapter ends in the middle of the deep.

The waters prevailed.

---

### The flood and Genesis 1 — a structural analysis of the un-creation

Genesis 7 cannot be read apart from Genesis 1. Every major element of the flood narrative is a direct inversion of a creation act:

| Genesis 1 — Creation | Genesis 7 — Un-creation |
|---|---|
| "The Spirit of God hovered over the face of the waters" (1:2) | The face of the waters swallows the face of the earth |
| God made the firmament and separated upper from lower waters (1:6-7) | The floodgates of the sky opened — the upper waters returned (7:11) |
| God gathered the lower waters and let dry land appear (1:9) | The waters covered all the high mountains — no dry land (7:19-20) |
| God filled the earth with living creatures (1:20-25) | Every creature that had the breath of life died (7:21-22) |
| God made the human in his image, breathed life into his nostrils (2:7) | Everything with breath in its nostrils died (7:22) |
| God saw all that he had made, and it was very good (1:31) | He wiped out every living thing on the face of the ground (7:23) |

The author of Genesis intended this inversion. The creation vocabulary is too consistent, too precise to be coincidental. The flood is not merely a natural disaster or even a judgment. It is a theological statement: the created order is not indestructible. The cosmos exists because God holds it in place. When God withdraws the order — when the separations fail, when the springs of the deep are permitted to burst and the floodgates of the sky are permitted to open — what remains is what was there at the beginning: the deep, the water, the dark.

The flood is the answering argument to creation. Creation said: *there can be a world*. The flood says: *it can also be unmade*. The covenant that will come in chapter 9 — with its rainbow and its promise "never again" — is the final word: *I will not unmake it again*. But this chapter does not have chapter 9 in it. This chapter lives entirely in the un-making.

---

### Verse 1 — "Go" and "I have seen"

**בֹּא אַתָּה (bo attah)** — "Go / Come in — you." The imperative is בֹּא (bo'), the second person singular of בּוֹא (bo'), "to come/enter." It is unusual in that it is most literally "come" rather than "go," but it functions as an entrance imperative: *enter*, *go in*. The verb puts Noah at the threshold. He has not yet crossed it.

**כִּי אֹתְךָ רָאִיתִי צַדִּיק לְפָנַי בַּדּוֹר הַזֶּה** — "for I have seen that you are righteous before me in this generation." God's seeing here is the counterpart to his seeing in 6:5 — where he saw that human wickedness was great. God sees the wickedness. God also sees the righteousness. Both acts of divine observation are given the same grammatical weight. The flood is not blind fury; it is sighted judgment and sighted grace, operating simultaneously.

**לְפָנַי (lefanai)** — "before me / in my presence." Noah's righteousness is not described as an abstract moral quality. It is located *in the face of God*, in the presence of the one who sees. This is the same relational category used in 6:11 — "the earth was corrupt before God's face" (לִפְנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים). The earth was corrupt in God's presence; Noah is righteous in God's presence. The language frames both the destruction and the salvation as personal, relational, covenantal — matters of face-to-face reality, not abstract cosmic mechanics.

---

### The number seven and the number forty

The chapter is saturated with the numbers that carry symbolic weight in the Hebrew tradition.

**שִׁבְעָה (shivah, seven)** — Seven pairs of clean animals (7:2, 7:3). Seven days of waiting before the flood (7:4, 7:10). Seven is the number of completeness, of the sabbath, of the world as God made it. The seven-day countdown to the flood is the last sabbath cycle before un-creation. After the seventh day, the deep bursts.

**אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם וְאַרְבָּעִים לַיְלָה (arba'im yom ve'arba'im layelah, forty days and forty nights)** — Forty is the number of trial, testing, and transformation in the Hebrew canon: forty years in the wilderness (Numbers 14:33), forty days of Moses on the mountain (Exodus 24:18), forty days of Elijah's journey to Horeb (1 Kings 19:8), forty days of Goliath's challenge (1 Samuel 17:16), forty days of Jonah's warning to Nineveh (Jonah 3:4), forty days of Jesus in the wilderness (Matthew 4:2). Forty is the span of a crisis that reshapes what comes after it. The forty days and nights of rain are not arbitrary. They are the span of a world-transformation.

The LXX preserves both numbers without alteration, reading **ἑπτά** (seven) and **τεσσαράκοντα ἡμέρας καὶ τεσσαράκοντα νύκτας** (forty days and forty nights). The Vaticanus manuscript does not harmonize or soften here. The numbers are too important to move.

---

### Summary table — key translation choices

| Verse | Hebrew | PNEUMA rendering | LXX (Vaticanus) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7:1 | בֹּא אַתָּה | "Go — you" | εἴσελθε σύ ("enter, you") | Entrance imperative at the threshold; the journey inward begins. |
| 7:1 | צַדִּיק לְפָנַי | "righteous before me" | δίκαιόν μου ("righteous of me") | Righteousness framed as relational presence, not abstract quality. |
| 7:4 | אֶמְחֶה (emcheh) | "I will wipe out" | ἐξαλείψω ("I will wipe out/erase") | Same verb as 6:7; the announcement becomes the act in 7:23. |
| 7:11 | תְּהוֹם רַבָּה | "the great deep" | ἀβύσσου ("the abyss") | תְּהוֹם of Gen 1:2 — the primordial chaos held back at creation, now released. |
| 7:11 | נִבְקְעוּ | "burst open" | ἐρράγησαν ("burst/broke open") | Violent verb — not a seeping but an eruption; the deep breaks out. |
| 7:11 | אֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם | "floodgates of the sky" | καταρράκται τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ("cataracts of heaven") | The firmament's windows unlatched; the upper waters of Gen 1:7 return. |
| 7:16 | וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה בַּעֲדוֹ | "Then the LORD shut the door behind him" | ἔκλεισεν κύριος ὁ θεὸς τὴν κιβωτὸν ἔξωθεν ("the Lord God shut the ark from outside") | God acts from outside — tenderest and most terrifying gesture in the chapter. |
| 7:18 | וַיִּגְבְּרוּ | "prevailed" | ἐπεκράτει ("dominated/prevailed") | גָּבַר — to be mighty, to rule; the waters are not rising, they are reigning. |
| 7:21 | וַיִּגְוַע | "perished / breathed their last" | ἀπέθανεν ("died") | גָּוַע = to expire, physiological — the breath leaving; LXX uses the generic death word. |
| 7:22 | נִשְׁמַת רוּחַ חַיִּים | "the breath of life in its nostrils" | πνοὴν ζωῆς ("breath of life") | Echo of Gen 2:7 — what God breathed in, the flood breathes out. |
| 7:23 | וַיִּמַּח | "He wiped out" | ἐξήλειψεν ("wiped out/erased") | מָחָה — to erase writing; the earth as inscription, now blank. |
| 7:23 | אַךְ נֹחַ | "Only Noah" | πλὴν Νωε ("only/except Noah") | אַךְ — absolute restriction; the sole remainder in a wiped world. |
| 7:24 | וַיִּגְבְּרוּ הַמַּיִם | "The waters prevailed" | ὑψώθη τὸ ὕδωρ ("the water was raised") | LXX softens to "raised"; MT's גָּבַר is dominion, not merely height. |

---

### A translator's note on the emotional register of this chapter

Genesis 7 requires the translator to do something uncomfortable: to slow down in the presence of mass death and stay there.

The temptation is to move through the death catalog quickly — to render it efficiently, to abbreviate it, to get to the rainbow. But the text does not move quickly. The Hebrew repeats itself with deliberate insistence. The animals come in two by two and are named and re-named. The date of the flood is given and repeated. The water rises in stages, each stage named, each named again. The dead are listed by category and then listed again. The text is doing this on purpose. It is refusing to let the flood become an abstraction.

Every translator of this chapter must decide how much weight to carry in verses 21–23. The death catalog is three verses long. In those three verses, the narrator describes the extinction of every living thing that breathes. The sentence "He wiped out every living thing on the face of the ground" is six Hebrew words. It describes the largest death in all of literary history. The translator who rushes through it is not following the Hebrew.

I have formatted verses 21–23 as a kind of broken poetry — not because the Hebrew is strictly poetic, but because the events described demand a different pace than prose. The line breaks are not in the manuscript. They are an interpretive choice: to force the reader to stop at each category, to hold each before going on. Bird. Animal. Beast. Swarming creature. Human being. Everything with breath. Everything on dry ground.

And then: Only Noah.

That contrast — the total and the singular — is the theological center of the chapter. The flood is total. The salvation is singular. One man, one family, one ark. Everything that had been made was unmade, except what the LORD had shut the door on.

The chapter ends at verse 24 without resolution, without comfort, without the voice of God. The waters prevailed one hundred and fifty days. Then the text is silent. There is nothing to say inside the prevailing. The next sound will come in chapter 8, when God remembers. But memory is not in this chapter. This chapter is the flood itself — the springs burst, the sky open, the waters rising, the breath leaving, the silence.

Translators who make this chapter sound tidy have not read it.

*— PNEUMA*
